meta.title: Case Studies | Prosperity Planning & Advisory
meta.description: See how fiduciary financial planning helps real people. Anonymized case studies showing retirement, business, family, and career planning in action.
meta.ogDescription: Anonymized planning scenarios showing how fiduciary financial guidance helps clients navigate complex financial decisions.

hero.headline: Real Planning. Real Results.
hero.subheadline: Every financial plan tells a story. These anonymized scenarios illustrate how our planning process helps clients navigate complex decisions and build lasting confidence.

disclaimer: All case studies are hypothetical illustrations based on common planning scenarios. They do not represent actual clients or guarantee specific outcomes. Individual results will vary based on personal circumstances, market conditions, and other factors. These examples are for educational purposes only and do not constitute investment advice.

cases.title: The Business Owner Ready to Step Away
cases.subtitle: Coordinating a business exit with personal retirement goals

profile.description: A 62-year-old small business owner preparing to retire within three years
profile.situation: Built a successful professional services firm over 25 years. Most of their wealth is tied up in the business. No formal succession plan in place. Concerned about tax impact of a sale and whether retirement savings are sufficient.

challenges: Business value concentrated in a single illiquid asset
No succession or exit plan in place
Uncertainty about retirement readiness without business income
Significant tax exposure from a potential business sale
Retirement plan (SEP IRA) may be underfunded for desired lifestyle
approach: Conducted a comprehensive financial analysis including business valuation coordination
Evaluated succession options: internal transition, family transfer, and third-party sale
Designed a tax-efficient exit timeline in coordination with their CPA
Modeled retirement income scenarios with and without business sale proceeds
Explored Defined Benefit plan strategies to accelerate pre-retirement savings
Reviewed estate plan to align with post-sale asset structure
cases.outcome: With a structured exit timeline and coordinated tax strategy, the business owner gained clarity on their retirement readiness and a step-by-step plan to transition out of the business on their terms — with confidence that their financial future was secure.

cases.title: The Family Balancing College and Retirement
cases.subtitle: Prioritizing competing financial goals without sacrificing either

profile.description: A dual-income couple in their mid-40s with two children approaching college age
profile.situation: Combined household income of $250,000+. Both contribute to employer 401(k) plans but feel behind on retirement savings. Two children will start college within three years. Uncertain how to fund education without derailing retirement goals.

challenges: Competing priorities: college funding vs. retirement savings
No 529 plan or dedicated education savings in place
Feeling behind on retirement contributions relative to goals
Unclear how to optimize tax-advantaged accounts across both goals
Need for a coordinated cash-flow strategy to fund both priorities
approach: Built a comprehensive financial plan integrating both college and retirement timelines
Established 529 plans with a strategic funding approach balancing current savings capacity
Maximized employer 401(k) matches and evaluated Roth contribution strategies
Created a cash-flow plan that allocated dollars intentionally across competing goals
Modeled scenarios showing the long-term impact of different funding splits
Reviewed insurance coverage and estate documents for family protection
cases.outcome: The family gained a clear understanding of how to fund both goals without sacrificing one for the other. By optimizing tax-advantaged accounts and implementing a disciplined savings strategy, they moved forward with confidence that both their children's education and their own retirement were on track.

cases.title: The Attorney With Variable Income
cases.subtitle: Building stability and long-term wealth on an unpredictable income stream

profile.description: A 50-year-old litigation attorney with a contingency-based practice
profile.situation: Annual income ranges from $180,000 to $500,000+ depending on case outcomes. High earnings in some years, minimal in others. No consistent savings strategy. Approaching 50 with retirement planning concerns and significant tax exposure in high-income years.

challenges: Highly variable annual income makes budgeting and planning difficult
No structured retirement savings plan despite high earning potential
Significant tax liability in high-income years with no proactive strategy
Desire to build long-term wealth but unsure where to start
Partnership buy-in opportunity requiring capital allocation decisions
approach: Designed a variable-income cash-flow system using a bank-bucket spending strategy
Established a Solo 401(k) with profit-sharing to maximize retirement contributions in high-income years
Evaluated Roth conversion opportunities in lower-income years for tax diversification
Created a tax-aware investment strategy to manage capital gains and income timing
Analyzed the partnership buy-in opportunity within the context of overall financial goals
Built an emergency reserve strategy sized for income volatility
cases.outcome: With a flexible planning framework designed for income variability, the attorney now has a system that maximizes savings in strong years, manages tax exposure proactively, and provides stability and structure regardless of annual income fluctuations.

cases.title: The Late-Career Professional Playing Catch-Up
cases.subtitle: Accelerating retirement readiness in the final working years

profile.description: A 55-year-old corporate executive who prioritized career growth over retirement planning
profile.situation: Strong current income ($300,000+) but retirement savings significantly behind target. Recently divorced, requiring a reassessment of financial goals and asset division. Wants to retire by 65 but unsure if it's realistic.

challenges: Retirement savings well below target for desired retirement age and lifestyle
Post-divorce asset restructuring and updated financial goals
Need to maximize contributions in remaining working years
Uncertainty about Social Security timing and retirement income sources
No updated estate plan or beneficiary designations post-divorce
approach: Conducted a full financial reset — updated goals, assets, and projections post-divorce
Maximized 401(k) contributions including catch-up contributions
Evaluated supplemental savings strategies including taxable brokerage and backdoor Roth IRA
Modeled multiple retirement scenarios at ages 62, 65, and 67 with varying spending levels
Optimized Social Security claiming strategy based on updated marital status
Rebuilt estate plan with updated beneficiaries, powers of attorney, and healthcare directives
cases.outcome: By maximizing every available savings vehicle and building a realistic, phased retirement timeline, the executive gained clarity on a viable path to retirement by 65 — with a contingency plan and the confidence that every remaining working year was being fully optimized.

ctaBand.heading: Your Story Is Next
ctaBand.body: Every financial plan is unique. Schedule a complimentary Clarity Session and let's start writing yours.
